title: Flail Chest with Pulmonary Contusion
chief_complaint: Motorcycle crash. Severe chest pain with breathing. Chest moving abnormally.

physical_exam.Chest: Flail segment: Paradoxical movement of left anterolateral chest wall (segments of ribs 4-7). Crepitus over fractured ribs. Severe tenderness.
physical_exam.Lungs: Decreased breath sounds left base. Crackles.
physical_exam.ATLS: A: patent, self-ventilating. B: tachypneic, hypoxic. C: tachycardic, hypotensive (hemorrhage + impaired venous return). D: alert, GCS 15.

hidden_data.cxr: Multiple left rib fractures (4th-7th ribs) with two fractures per rib in lateral position (flail segment). Left pulmonary contusion (patchy alveolar infiltrate). No pneumothorax. No hemothorax.
hidden_data.ct_chest: Left pulmonary contusion involving 30% of lung volume. Flail segment confirmed. Small hemothorax (not requiring drainage). Scapula fracture.
hidden_data.abg: pH 7.30, PaCO2 55, PaO2 48, SaO2 82%. Hypercapnic respiratory failure.
hidden_data.labs: Hb 9.5 (blood loss from rib fractures - each rib can lose 100-150mL)

correct_diagnosis: Flail Chest (Left 4th-7th Ribs) with Pulmonary Contusion and Acute Respiratory Failure
key_learning_points: Flail chest = ≥3 adjacent ribs fractured in ≥2 places → free-floating segment moves paradoxically (inward with inspiration, outward with expiration). Underlying pulmonary contusion causes the morbidity, NOT the flail segment itself

Paradoxical movement impairs ventilation → atelectasis → V/Q mismatch → hypoxia. Pain from fractures → splinting → hypoventilation → hypercapnia → respiratory failure. Pulmonary contusion develops over 24-48h → worsening hypoxia

Management: 1) Oxygen, non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) if not in respiratory failure. 2) Aggressive analgesia: IV opioids (Morphine PCA), Epidural analgesia (gold standard - allows deep breathing, clearing secretions). 3) Pulmonary toilet: Incentive spirometry, chest physiotherapy, suctioning. 4) Intubation and mechanical ventilation if: Respiratory failure (PaCO2 >55, PaO2 <60 despite O2), Severe contusion (>30% lung), Refractory shock, Multiple injuries

Surgical fixation (ORIF ribs): Indicated for flail chest with respiratory failure, severe pain despite optimal analgesia, inability to wean from ventilator. Reduces ventilator days, ICU stay, pneumonia, and improves long-term outcomes

Complications: Pneumonia (most common - 30%), ARDS, Chest wall deformity, Chronic pain, Nonunion of ribs. Mortality 10-20% depending on associated injuries and age. Pulmonary contusion resolves over 7-10 days
